fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to ship the modeltyper client-contract generator. The basic idea is: given a server-side data model, spit out TypeScript definitions that frontend devs can actually use without hand-writing interfaces. We've been burned too many times by stale types on the client side.

The tool needs to handle the usual stuff — columns, computed attributes (both the old-style and new accessor style), relationships, and those backed string constant sets we use for roles and similar things. There should be a way to wrap everything in a global namespace for projects that need it, and also a raw JSON metadata mode for tooling integrations.

For the constant sets specifically, remember we had that escaping issue in the auth module last time — make sure backslash-heavy values don't get mangled. Also the enum output style should be switchable between two modes (the new one uses a different type alias pattern).

We also need the discovery and inspection endpoints so downstream tooling knows which models are even valid targets — abstract or missing models should fail gracefully with a normalized error token, not a stack trace.

One thing I'm not 100% sure about: the fillable subset feature — I think it generates a Pick<> type but I forget the exact suffix convention. Check how we handled optional/hidden fields in the previous field-rendering spike. Four concrete sample models should be enough to exercise all the paths.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: generate_definitions(subject: string, options: GenerationOptions, config?: GenerationConfig): string

trigger_keywords: how many models are discovered
which models are eligible for generation
what concrete models does discovery return
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Discovery returns exactly 4 concrete models in this order: Complex, Pivot, User, Team. The output format is 'count=4' on the first line followed by one 'model=<Name>' line per model. Abstract or missing subjects return 'result=null'.

trigger_keywords: what does inspect_model return for user
how many attributes does the user model have
what is the table name and relation count for user
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Inspecting the 'user' subject returns: result=inspected, table=users, attributes=12, relations=1. The attribute count of 12 includes both persisted columns and computed mutators.

trigger_keywords: how should hidden or optional fields be marked
which user fields are optional
what fields use the question mark modifier
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Fields that are hidden or guarded are marked optional with '?' suffix on the field name (e.g., 'password?: string'). Nullable fields append '| null' to the type. A field can be both optional and nullable simultaneously (e.g., 'remember_token?: string | null'). For the User model, 'password' and 'remember_token' are the optional fields.

trigger_keywords: how does the const object enum style differ from the use_enums style
what is the difference between default enum output and use_enums flag
what type alias pattern does each enum mode produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Default (no use_enums): emits a 'const Roles = { ... } as const;' object followed by 'export type Roles = typeof Roles[keyof typeof Roles]'. With use_enums=true: emits 'export const enum Roles { ... }' followed by 'export type RolesEnum = `${Roles}`'. The interface field type also changes: default uses 'Roles', use_enums mode uses 'RolesEnum'.

trigger_keywords: how should backslashes in enum values be escaped
the USERCLASS constant has backslashes, how are they handled
what is the correct string literal for the App\Models\User enum value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Backslash characters inside string constant values must be double-escaped in the TypeScript output. The value 'App\Models\User' must appear as 'App\\Models\\User' in the emitted TypeScript literal. This applies to both the const-object style and the const-enum style outputs.

trigger_keywords: how does global namespace wrapping work
what is the exact structure of the namespace output
what lines wrap the interface when global option is true
what does the fillable subset option produce
how is the Pick type generated for editable fields
what suffix is used for the fillable type alias
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When fillable_subset=true, a 'export type <ModelName><fillable_suffix> = Pick<ModelName, ...>' line is appended after the interface closing brace. The fillable_suffix comes from the options (e.g., 'Editable'). For the User model the fillable fields are: 'name', 'email', 'password', 'role_traditional', 'role_new'.

trigger_keywords: how should missing or abstract models fail
what error token is returned when the subject is not found
what is the normalized error output for invalid subjects
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When generation is requested for a subject that does not exist or is abstract, the adapter must print 'error=no_models_found\n' to stdout. No exception stack trace should be surfaced. Both 'missing_sample' and 'abstract_sample' trigger this error.

trigger_keywords: how does JSON metadata output structure enums
what is the shape of the json output for generate_definitions
what keys appear in the json metadata response
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: JSON metadata output contains three top-level keys: 'interfaces' (object keyed by model name, value is array of {name, type} objects), 'relations' (array of objects keyed by type string, each containing {name, type}), and 'enums' (array of objects keyed by enum name, each containing {name, type} where type is the full declaration string). With use_enums=true the interface field types use the 'RolesEnum' suffix form. The JSON is pretty-printed with 4-space indentation.

trigger_keywords: how does the accessor probe work
how are new-style computed attributes detected
what selector format does the accessor resolver use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The accessor_probe request accepts a 'selector' string and returns 'accessor=<selector>\nresult=found\n' if the computed attribute is resolvable on the target model. The probe must recognize both modern accessor style and legacy mutator style. The selector 'new_role_value' is a valid resolvable accessor on the User model.

trigger_keywords: how are database column types mapped to TypeScript types
what TypeScript type does a json or jsonb column produce
what type is used for binary or unknown column types
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Type mapping rules: integer/bigInteger/decimal/double/float/mediumInteger/smallInteger/year → number; char/string/text/longText/mediumText/enum/ipAddress/macAddress/date/dateTime/immutableDateTime/immutableCustomDateTime/immutableDate/time/timestamp/uuid/ulid → string; boolean → boolean; json/jsonb → Record<string, unknown>; binary and any unrecognized type → unknown. Custom casts that don't match a known category also map to unknown.

trigger_keywords: how does map_type_token handle case sensitivity
is the type token lookup case-sensitive or case-insensitive
the type token B with mapping b=2, what does it return
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Type token lookup is case-insensitive. The token 'B' matches the mapping key 'b' and returns 'type=2\n'. The adapter normalizes the incoming token to lowercase before performing the lookup.

trigger_keywords: what interface sections are output and in what order
what comment headers separate the field groups in the interface
how are columns, mutators, and relations separated in the output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The interface body is split into three commented sections in this order: '// columns' (persisted database columns), '// mutators' (computed attributes / accessors), '// relations' (relationship fields). Each section header appears only when that section has at least one field. Relations are omitted if no relationships exist on the model.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

"

context_pointers.vague_reference: that escaping issue in the auth module last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The USERCLASS enum constant value 'App\Models\User' requires double-backslash escaping to 'App\\Models\\User' in TypeScript string literals; see feature3_1_enum_rendering.json and feature2_1_default_interface_output.json expected outputs

context_pointers.vague_reference: the old-style and new accessor style
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Two computed attribute detection mechanisms: legacy PHP-style getMutatorAttribute methods and modern Attribute accessor objects; both must be probed and counted in the attributes total (12 for User); see feature5_2_accessor_resolution.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the fillable subset feature — I think it generates a Pick<> type but I forget the exact suffix convention
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The fillable_subset option appends 'export type <Model><fillable_suffix> = Pick<Model, ...>' using the fillable_suffix option value (e.g., 'Editable'); fillable fields for User are name, email, password, role_traditional, role_new; see feature4_1_generation_options.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: Check how we handled optional/hidden fields in the previous field-rendering spike
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Hidden model fields (password, remember_token on User) are rendered with '?' optional marker; nullable fields append '| null'; see feature2_1_default_interface_output.json and feature3_2_relationship_rendering.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the new one uses a different type alias pattern
context_pointers.actual_meaning: use_enums=true switches from 'const ... as const' + 'typeof ... [keyof typeof ...]' pattern to 'export const enum' + template literal type 'export type RolesEnum = `${Roles}`'; interface field types change from 'Roles' to 'RolesEnum'; see feature4_1_generation_options.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have full context on right now — you'd need to dig into the existing test cases or the sample model definitions to confirm the exact behavior. Check the rcb_tests/public_test_cases directory for the relevant fixture.

feature1_1_model_discovery.json.description: Discovers all eligible sample data models in the project or narrows discovery to a requested model.

feature1_2_model_inspection.json.description: Inspects a sample data model and reports whether schema attributes and relationships are available for generation.

feature2_1_default_interface_output.json.description: Generates a TypeScript interface for a sample user-shaped data model, including columns, computed fields, relationships, and literal union constants.

feature2_2_complex_type_mapping_output.j.description: Generates a TypeScript interface for a model containing many database field categories and maps each field to the corresponding TypeScript type.

cases.expected_output:
export interface Complex {
  // columns
  id: number
  big_integer: number
  binary: unknown
  boolean: boolean
  char: string
  date_time: string
  immutable_date_time: string
  immutable_custom_date_time: string
  date: string
  immutable_date: string
  decimal: number
  double: number
  enum: string
  float: number
  integer: number
  ip_address: string
  json: Record<string, unknown>
  jsonb: Record<string, unknown>
  long_text: string
  mac_address: string
  medium_integer: number
  medium_text: string
  small_integer: number
  string: string
  casted_uppercase_string: unknown
  text: string
  time: string
  timestamp: string
  year: number
  uuid: string
  ulid: string
  created_at: string | null
  updated_at: string | null
  deleted_at: string | null
}

feature2_3_global_namespace_output.json.description: Wraps generated interfaces in a configured global namespace when namespace output is requested.

feature2_4_json_metadata_output.json.description: Generates structured JSON metadata for a sample model when JSON output is requested, including interface fields, relations, and enum metadata.

feature3_1_enum_rendering.json.description: Renders backed string constants as TypeScript declarations while preserving descriptions and escaped string values.

feature3_2_relationship_rendering.json.description: Renders relationship fields as TypeScript properties or structured field metadata, with optionality and plural resource-name variants.

feature4_1_generation_options.json.description: Applies generation switches that alter the emitted interface, including editable subsets, enum style, plural names, relation inclusion, hidden fields, date object timestamps, nullable optionality, and API resource wrapping.

feature4_2_type_mapping_configuration.js.description: Maps raw type tokens using default and user-provided mappings, including nullable tokens, unknown tokens, timestamp date-object mapping, and custom overrides.

feature5_1_error_normalization.json.description: Reports language-neutral error categories when generation cannot find models, a type token is empty, or an accessor selector cannot be resolved.

feature5_2_accessor_resolution.json.description: Recognizes both modern and legacy computed attribute selectors on a sample model.
